en.history: Withania somnifera has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for over 3,000 years, classified in classical texts such as the Charaka Samhita as a "rasayana" (rejuvenative) herb for building strength, vitality, and resilience to stress.
en.features: Classified as an adaptogen -- a class of herbs traditionally used to help the body adapt to physical and mental stress. Most modern research has focused on its effects on stress, anxiety, and cortisol levels.
pros: A 2024 systematic review and meta-analysis of 9 randomized controlled trials (558 participants) found significant reductions in perceived stress, anxiety scores, and serum cortisol compared to placebo.
Multiple double-blind, placebo-controlled trials report improved sleep quality and stress-related symptoms within 8 weeks.
cons: Can interact with thyroid medication and may raise thyroid hormone levels -- caution advised for anyone with a thyroid condition.
Not recommended during pregnancy.
Most trials are short-term (8-12 weeks), so long-term safety data is limited; some trials reported mild-to-moderate adverse events.

references.title: Effects of Ashwagandha (Withania Somnifera) on stress and anxiety: A systematic review and meta-analysis
Adaptogenic and Anxiolytic Effects of Ashwagandha Root Extract in Healthy Adults: A Double-blind, Randomized, Placebo-controlled Clinical Study

en.history: A cornerstone of both Ayurvedic and Traditional Chinese Medicine for thousands of years, turmeric has long been used both as a culinary spice and medicinally for digestive complaints, wound healing, and inflammatory conditions across South Asia.
en.features: Curcumin, its primary active compound, has documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity and is the most-studied component of the root.
pros: A network meta-analysis of 17 studies found turmeric preparations significantly reduced knee osteoarthritis pain (WOMAC scores).
Randomized, double-blind trials show measurable reductions in inflammatory markers alongside standard osteoarthritis care.
cons: Curcumin has poor natural bioavailability on its own -- it's often paired with piperine (black pepper extract) to improve absorption.
Generally less effective than ibuprofen for direct pain relief in head-to-head comparisons, though appears safer for short-term use.
May increase bleeding risk when combined with blood-thinning medication; high doses can cause GI upset.

references.title: Effect of turmeric products on knee osteoarthritis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
Efficacy and safety of curcumin and its combination with boswellic acid in osteoarthritis: a comparative, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study

en.history: Used since ancient Egyptian, Greek, and Roman times as a calming remedy for digestive complaints and as a mild sedative, chamomile remains one of the most widely consumed herbal teas globally, long associated with soothing sleep and stomach upset.
en.features: Most researched for its calming effects -- primarily studied for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) and sleep quality rather than everyday situational stress.
pros: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 randomized trials found significant improvement in GAD symptoms (HAM-A scale) after 2-4 weeks, and improved sleep quality.
Widely regarded as a gentle, low-risk option compared to many other calming herbs.
cons: Evidence for general (non-GAD) anxiety and for insomnia specifically is mixed and inconsistent across trials.
Can cause allergic reactions in people sensitive to ragweed, daisies, or related plants in the Asteraceae family.
May interact with blood-thinning medication.

references.title: Therapeutic efficacy and safety of chamomile for state anxiety, generalized anxiety disorder, insomnia, and sleep quality: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials and quasi-randomized trials
The Effect of Oral Chamomile on Anxiety: A Systematic Review of Clinical Trials

en.history: Zingiber officinale has been used for thousands of years in traditional Chinese and Ayurvedic medicine as a digestive aid and for settling an upset stomach, and by the Middle Ages had spread widely through trade as both a culinary spice and a folk remedy for nausea and travel sickness.
en.features: Contains gingerols and shogaols, pungent compounds thought to affect gut motility and serotonin (5-HT3) receptor pathways involved in nausea signaling. Modern research has focused heavily on nausea and vomiting (chemotherapy-related, postoperative, and pregnancy-related) and, more recently, on joint pain in osteoarthritis.
pros: A systematic review and meta-analysis of chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting trials (18 articles) found low-dose ginger (≤1 g/day for more than 3 days) associated with a 60% reduction in the likelihood of acute vomiting compared with control, though no significant effect was found on overall nausea severity or delayed vomiting.

A meta-analysis of 5 randomized, placebo-controlled trials in osteoarthritis patients (593 participants) found ginger produced statistically significant, though modest, reductions in pain and disability compared with placebo (Bartels et al., Osteoarthritis and Cartilage, 2015).
Multiple randomized trials support ginger's use for postoperative and pregnancy-related nausea, generally at doses around 1 g/day.
cons: Can enhance the effect of anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications (e.g., warfarin) by inhibiting platelet aggregation -- evidence on real-world bleeding risk is mixed, but caution and closer INR monitoring are generally advised for anyone on blood thinners.
In the osteoarthritis meta-analysis, patients taking ginger were roughly twice as likely to discontinue treatment due to adverse events (mainly gastrointestinal) compared with placebo.
Effect sizes for pain and nausea are generally modest, and results vary by preparation, dose, and duration across trials.

references.title: Efficacy of Ginger (Zingiber officinale) in Ameliorating Chemotherapy-Induced Nausea and Vomiting and Chemotherapy-Related Outcomes: A Systematic Review Update and Meta-Analysis
Efficacy and safety of ginger in osteoarthritis patients: a meta-analysis of randomized placebo-controlled trials

en.history: Valeriana officinalis root has a long history of traditional use in Europe as a calming remedy for nervous tension and sleeplessness, dating back to ancient Greek and Roman medicine and continuing through European herbal traditions into the present.
en.features: Contains valerenic acid and related compounds thought to interact with GABA-A receptor pathways, the same general system targeted by prescription sedatives, which is the proposed basis for its traditional use as a calming, sleep-supportive herb. Most modern research has focused on subjective sleep quality in people with mild insomnia.
pros: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 16 randomized, placebo-controlled trials (about 1,093 participants) found that six trials reporting dichotomous sleep-quality outcomes showed a statistically significant benefit (relative risk of improvement 1.8, 95% CI 1.2-2.9), with valerian described as generally well tolerated (Bent et al., American Journal of Medicine, 2006).

Across trials, valerian is consistently reported as having a favorable side-effect profile similar to placebo, which supports its traditional use as a gentler option than sedative medications for some users.
cons: Evidence for objective sleep improvement is inconsistent -- the same meta-analysis noted significant heterogeneity between trials and evidence of publication bias, and other reviews have concluded the evidence for sleep-promoting effects remains inconclusive when only higher-quality trials are considered.

Has a theoretical and pharmacologically plausible risk of additive sedation when combined with benzodiazepines, barbiturates, alcohol, or other sedative medications; caution and medical guidance are advised before combining.
Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding -- possible risks to the fetus or infant have not been adequately evaluated in clinical studies.

references.title: Valerian for sleep: a systematic review and meta-analysis
Valerian - Health Professional Fact Sheet (NIH Office of Dietary Supplements)

en.history: Mentha piperita has been used since antiquity across Greek, Roman, and Egyptian medicine as a digestive remedy, and enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules remain one of the most studied botanical treatments for functional digestive complaints today.
en.features: Menthol, its principal active compound, acts as a smooth-muscle relaxant via calcium-channel blockade in the gut, which is the proposed mechanism behind its traditional use for bloating, cramping, and general digestive discomfort. Topically, menthol's cooling, muscle-relaxing effect is also the basis for its studied use in tension-type headache.
pros: A meta-analysis of 12 randomized controlled trials (835 patients) found enteric-coated peppermint oil associated with a 2.39-fold greater likelihood of global symptom improvement and a 1.78-fold greater likelihood of abdominal pain relief compared with placebo in irritable bowel syndrome, with a number needed to treat of 3-4 (Alammar et al., BMC Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2019).

Randomized, placebo-controlled trials of a 10% peppermint oil solution applied topically to the forehead and temples have found it significantly more effective than placebo for tension-type headache, with one early crossover trial finding no significant difference versus 1,000 mg acetaminophen.
cons: The same smooth-muscle-relaxing action that helps the gut can relax the lower esophageal sphincter -- peppermint (especially non-enteric-coated forms) may worsen symptoms in people with GERD or frequent heartburn, so it is generally not recommended for that group.
Enteric-coated capsules are specifically preferred in trials for this reason; uncoated oil or peppermint tea is more likely to trigger reflux-type discomfort in sensitive individuals.
Most positive trial evidence is for standardized enteric-coated oil capsules at specific doses -- effects from peppermint tea or casual culinary use are far less studied and likely weaker.

references.title: The impact of peppermint oil on the irritable bowel syndrome: a meta-analysis of the pooled clinical data
[Peppermint oil in the acute treatment of tension-type headache]

en.history: Silybum marianum has been used since Greco-Roman antiquity as a traditional remedy for liver and gallbladder complaints, and its extract silymarin became a standard subject of European phytotherapy research through the 20th century as a hepatoprotective agent.
en.features: Silymarin, a complex of flavonolignans (including silybin) extracted from the seeds, is studied for antioxidant, free-radical-scavenging, and hepatocyte membrane-stabilizing activity. Modern research has focused mainly on markers of liver enzyme levels in fatty liver disease and drug- or toxin-induced liver stress.
pros: A 2024 systematic review and meta-analysis of 26 randomized controlled trials (2,375 patients) found silymarin associated with reductions in ALT, AST, cholesterol, and triglycerides, along with improved insulin sensitivity and hepatic steatosis on histology, in patients with NAFLD/NASH, though the authors noted results need confirmation by further research (Li et al., Annals of Hepatology, 2024).

A meta-analysis of 5 randomized controlled trials (1,198 patients) found silymarin significantly reduced the occurrence of antituberculosis drug-induced liver injury at week 4 compared with placebo (relative risk 0.33).
cons: Silymarin has notably poor oral bioavailability (roughly 20-50% absorption) due to low water solubility and extensive first-pass metabolism, so effects can vary widely between standard and specially formulated (e.g., micellar or phospholipid-complexed) extracts.

Laboratory research suggests silymarin's flavonolignans can exhibit weak estrogen-receptor-binding (phytoestrogenic) activity; while human data have not shown a reproducible rise in circulating estrogen at typical doses, caution is generally advised for people with hormone-sensitive conditions or on hormone-modulating therapy.
Trials for chronic hepatitis C have generally shown no significant benefit on viral load, indicating silymarin's liver-supportive effects do not extend to all liver conditions equally.

references.title: Administration of silymarin in NAFLD/NASH: A systematic review and meta-analysis
Prophylactic Therapy of Silymarin (Milk Thistle) on Antituberculosis Drug-Induced Liver Injury: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials

en.history: Sambucus nigra berries and flowers have a long history of traditional use across European folk medicine for colds, flu, and respiratory complaints, and standardized elderberry extracts have become a widely studied over-the-counter option for seasonal immune support in recent decades.
en.features: Elderberry is rich in anthocyanins and flavonoids studied for antiviral and immunomodulatory activity, including effects on cytokine production and immune cell signaling. Modern research has focused mainly on the duration and severity of upper respiratory symptoms such as cold and flu.
pros: A meta-analysis of randomized, controlled clinical trials (180 participants total) found black elderberry supplementation associated with a substantial reduction in upper respiratory symptoms, with a notably large effect size (Hawkins et al., Complementary Therapies in Medicine, 2019).
A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of 312 economy-class air travelers found elderberry extract associated with reduced cold duration and severity for those who did become ill.

A separate systematic review of 5 randomized trials concluded elderberry was safe for prevention or treatment of viral respiratory illness, reporting no evidence that it overstimulates the immune system in the healthy populations studied.
cons: Because elderberry's proposed mechanism includes stimulating cytokine production and immune activity, it carries a theoretical concern for people with autoimmune conditions, where the immune system is already overactive -- a published case report described a woman with pre-existing Hashimoto's thyroiditis who developed severe autoimmune hepatitis while taking long-term elderberry supplements, which resolved after stopping elderberry and starting steroids.

Most positive trial evidence comes from healthy adults or people with acute viral infections, not from people with chronic autoimmune disease, so safety in that specific group is much less established.
Raw, unripe, or uncooked elderberry (as opposed to properly prepared commercial extracts) can cause gastrointestinal upset and should be avoided.

references.title: Black elderberry (Sambucus nigra) supplementation effectively treats upper respiratory symptoms: A meta-analysis of randomized, controlled clinical trials
A Plausible Association Between the Use of Elderberry and Autoimmune Hepatitis

en.history: Echinacea purpurea is native to North America, where Plains tribes used the plant topically and internally for infections, wounds, and pain long before it was adopted in Europe; it became a widely used cold remedy in Germany in the 20th century and remains one of the most common herbal supplements in the US today.
en.features: Contains alkylamides, polysaccharides, and caffeic acid derivatives thought to modulate innate immune activity. Most modern research has focused narrowly on whether standardized extracts shorten or prevent common cold episodes, rather than on broader immune-boosting claims.
pros: A 2012 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial found that consistent use of an Echinacea purpurea extract over four months reduced the total number of cold episodes, with the strongest preventive effect against colds caused by enveloped viruses, and safety comparable to placebo.

A 2014 Cochrane systematic review of 24 double-blind trials (4,631 participants) found that prevention trials tended toward a small relative risk reduction (roughly 10-20%), though the authors described the clinical relevance as uncertain.
cons: The same Cochrane review found that treatment trials (started after cold symptoms began) showed only weak, mostly non-significant effects on symptom duration or severity.

European regulatory guidance advises against use in people with autoimmune conditions, multiple sclerosis, or on immunosuppressive therapy, based on echinacea's T-cell-stimulating mechanism -- though documented case reports of actual disease flares remain sparse.
Trial results vary considerably by echinacea species, plant part, and preparation used, so findings from one product don't necessarily generalize to another.

references.title: Safety and Efficacy Profile of Echinacea purpurea to Prevent Common Cold Episodes: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial
Echinacea for preventing and treating the common cold (Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews)

en.history: Ginkgo biloba is among the oldest living tree species, and its leaves and seeds have been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries; the standardized leaf extract (EGb 761) used in most modern research was developed in Europe in the latter half of the 20th century.
en.features: Standardized extracts are concentrated for flavonoid glycosides and terpene lactones (ginkgolides, bilobalide), compounds studied for effects on cerebral and peripheral blood flow and for inhibiting platelet-activating factor. Research has concentrated on cognitive outcomes in impaired populations and on walking distance in circulation-related leg pain.
pros: A 2016 systematic review and meta-analysis of 21 randomized controlled trials found that ginkgo biloba extract, combined with conventional treatment, produced modest improvements in cognitive scores and activities of daily living in people with mild cognitive impairment or Alzheimer's disease.

Earlier meta-analyses of placebo-controlled trials reported a modest increase in pain-free walking distance in people with intermittent claudication (circulation-related leg pain), broadly consistent with its traditional use for circulatory support.
cons: A 2013 Cochrane review of 14 trials concluded there is no evidence of a clinically significant benefit for intermittent claudication, despite a small statistical improvement in walking distance.

A meta-analysis restricted to healthy (non-impaired) adults found effect sizes near zero for memory, attention, and executive function, suggesting any benefit is concentrated in people with existing impairment rather than general cognitive enhancement.

Case reports and a large Veterans Administration population analysis have linked concurrent ginkgo and warfarin use to increased bleeding risk (hazard ratio 1.38), so caution is warranted for anyone on anticoagulant medication, even though controlled pharmacology studies have not consistently shown an effect on coagulation.

references.title: Ginkgo Biloba for Mild Cognitive Impairment and Alzheimer's Disease: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials
Ginkgo biloba for intermittent claudication (Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews)

en.history: Ocimum tenuiflorum (Tulsi) has been revered in Ayurvedic tradition and Hindu culture for well over 3,000 years, traditionally grown near homes and temples and used for respiratory, digestive, and stress-related complaints.
en.features: Classified as an adaptogen. Contains eugenol, ursolic acid, and rosmarinic acid, compounds studied for effects on the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and cortisol, and separately on glucose and lipid metabolism.
pros: A 2022 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 100 adults found that 250 mg/day of a standardized holy basil extract over 8 weeks produced a significantly greater reduction in perceived stress (37% vs 19% with placebo) and improved sleep-related scores.

A 2017 systematic review of 24 human studies concluded that tulsi consistently showed favorable effects on stress, metabolic, and immune markers across trial designs, with no significant adverse events reported.
cons: Animal studies have found that high doses may interfere with implantation and full-term pregnancy and may affect fertility markers such as sperm count and sex hormones; it is not recommended during pregnancy.
May increase bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulant medications; common guidance is to stop use at least two weeks before a scheduled surgery.
Most positive human trials used small samples and standardized proprietary extracts over 6-8 weeks, so results may not generalize to raw leaf tea or long-term use.

references.title: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial investigating the effects of an Ocimum tenuiflorum (Holy Basil) extract (Holixer) on stress, mood, and sleep in adults experiencing stress
The Clinical Efficacy and Safety of Tulsi in Humans: A Systematic Review of the Literature

en.history: Glycyrrhiza glabra root has a documented history of use across ancient Egyptian, Greek, Chinese, and Ayurvedic medicine, traditionally chewed, brewed, or gargled as a soothing remedy for throat irritation, cough, and digestive complaints.
en.features: Contains glycyrrhizin, which is metabolized to glycyrrhetinic acid, along with flavonoids studied for anti-inflammatory and mucosa-protective effects on throat and gastric tissue. Glycyrrhizin's inhibition of the enzyme that normally breaks down cortisol is the mechanism behind both its soothing effects and its main safety concerns.
pros: A 2019 systematic review and meta-analysis of 5 randomized controlled trials (609 patients) found that topical or gargled licorice before intubation reduced the incidence and severity of postoperative sore throat compared with non-analgesic controls.
A randomized trial of preoperative licorice lozenges in smokers undergoing general anesthesia found postextubation cough incidence of 24% in the licorice group versus 52% in the control group.
cons: Regular high intake of glycyrrhizin-containing licorice is associated with pseudoaldosteronism -- sodium and water retention with potassium loss -- which can raise blood pressure, a concern for anyone with hypertension.
Higher glycyrrhizin exposure during pregnancy (over roughly 500 mg/week) has been associated with increased risk of preterm birth, so it is generally advised against during pregnancy.

Most of the positive throat/cough evidence comes from topical gargles, lozenges, or standardized extracts rather than whole-root tea or loose supplements, so results may not transfer directly to every preparation.

references.title: Topical application of licorice for prevention of postoperative sore throat in adults: A systematic review and meta-analysis
Effect of preoperative licorice lozenges on incidence of postextubation cough and sore throat in smokers undergoing general anesthesia and endotracheal intubation

en.history: Urtica dioica has a long history in European folk medicine as a spring tonic, diuretic, and nutrient-dense food, and was traditionally applied directly to the skin over painful joints ("urtication") -- a practice that predates its modern study for joint pain.
en.features: Leaves are rich in flavonoids (quercetin, rutin) and carotenoids and have been studied for COX-1/COX-2-inhibiting, anti-inflammatory activity; the root is studied separately for urinary symptoms. Human research on allergy and joint pain has focused on these anti-inflammatory and mild antihistamine-like actions.
pros: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial (27 patients) found that topical nettle leaf applied to osteoarthritic thumb/index-finger pain produced significantly greater reductions in pain and disability scores than a placebo leaf.

A 2017 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of oral stinging nettle root extract in allergic rhinitis found a significant reduction in nasal eosinophil count relative to placebo, alongside symptom improvement in both groups.
cons: In that same allergic rhinitis trial, overall symptom-severity scores improved similarly in both the nettle and placebo groups, so the evidence for a clear symptomatic advantage over placebo is mixed.

Nettle leaf contains substantial vitamin K, which may counteract the effect of anticoagulant medications such as warfarin -- caution and monitoring are advised for anyone on blood-thinning medication.
Much of the joint-pain evidence comes from small, older studies (fewer than 30 participants), so confidence in the effect size is limited.

references.title: Efficacy of Supportive Therapy of Allergic Rhinitis by Stinging Nettle (Urtica dioica) root extract: a Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled, Clinical Trial
Randomized controlled trial of nettle sting for treatment of base-of-thumb pain

en.history: Taraxacum officinale has a long history of use across European, North American, and traditional Chinese medicine as a mild diuretic and digestive bitter, historically taken as a tea or tincture from the leaf and root to support urination and appetite.
en.features: The leaf is notably rich in potassium and is traditionally valued as a diuretic that (unlike many pharmaceutical diuretics) replaces some of the potassium it helps the body excrete; the root is more often used as a bitter to stimulate digestive secretions. Modern research is still limited but has begun testing the traditional diuretic claim directly in humans.
pros: A 2009 pilot study in the Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine (17 healthy volunteers) found that a fresh dandelion leaf extract significantly increased urinary frequency and the urine excretion ratio within 5 hours of a single dose compared with baseline.

Traditional use as a mild digestive bitter for occasional bloating and sluggish digestion is long-documented across multiple herbal traditions, and small experimental studies suggest choleretic (bile-stimulating) activity that may support this use.
cons: Clinical evidence is still very limited -- the human diuretic pilot study had no placebo control and only 17 participants, so it does not by itself establish a reliable effect.

Dandelion leaf is high in potassium; combined with its diuretic activity, this may pose a hyperkalemia risk for people with impaired kidney function, so it is not recommended for anyone with significant kidney disease.
May have additive effects with diuretic or blood-sugar-lowering medications, and can cause allergic reactions in people sensitive to related plants (ragweed, daisies, chamomile).

references.title: The diuretic effect in human subjects of an extract of Taraxacum officinale folium over a single day
The Role of Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale) in Liver Health and Hepatoprotective Properties

en.history: Crataegus species (hawthorn) have been used in European herbal medicine since at least the 19th century as a cardiac tonic, and earlier in traditional Chinese medicine (as "shan zha") for digestive and circulatory support; it remains one of the more extensively studied cardiovascular botanicals.
en.features: Hawthorn's leaves, flowers, and berries contain flavonoids and oligomeric procyanidins thought to have antioxidant activity and mild effects on blood vessel tone and heart muscle contractility. Most modern clinical research has focused on standardized extracts (e.g., WS 1442) in mild-to-moderate chronic heart failure and, more recently, blood pressure.
pros: A 2025 meta-analysis of 6 randomized, placebo-controlled trials (428 participants) found hawthorn extract produced a statistically significant reduction in systolic blood pressure (mean difference of roughly 6.65 mmHg) compared with placebo.

A 2008 Cochrane systematic review of 14 trials concluded hawthorn extract, used as an add-on to standard treatment, produced significant improvements in exercise tolerance and symptoms such as fatigue and shortness of breath in patients with chronic heart failure, with infrequent, mild, and transient adverse events.
cons: The larger, longer HERB-CHF randomized trial (120 patients, 6 months) found no significant benefit over placebo on 6-minute walk distance, peak oxygen uptake, or quality of life in NYHA class II-III heart failure, so evidence for meaningful functional benefit is mixed, especially over the longer term.

Because hawthorn may have additive effects on heart contractility and blood pressure, anyone taking cardiac medications (e.g., digoxin, beta-blockers, antihypertensives, blood thinners) should only use it under physician supervision -- an early pharmacokinetic study found no significant change in digoxin levels, but hawthorn compounds can interfere with digoxin immunoassay readings and theoretical pharmacodynamic interaction remains a concern.
Should not be used as a substitute for prescribed heart failure or blood pressure medication without medical guidance.

references.title: Hawthorn (Crataegus spp.) Clinically Significantly Reduces Blood Pressure in Hypertension: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Placebo-Controlled Clinical Trials
Interaction study between digoxin and a preparation of hawthorn (Crataegus oxyacantha)

en.history: Lepidium meyenii has been cultivated in the high Andes of Peru for thousands of years, traditionally used as a nutrient-dense food and taken to support energy, stamina, and fertility.
en.features: Maca is a cruciferous root vegetable containing macamides and macaenes, compounds unique to the plant that are of ongoing research interest for hormonal and sexual-function effects. Most clinical research has focused on sexual dysfunction, menopausal symptoms, and, more recently, antioxidant activity, rather than energy or mood directly.
pros: A systematic review (BMC Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2010) of 4 randomized controlled trials (131 participants) found maca showed a beneficial effect on sexual dysfunction and libido in some trials, including in healthy adult men and menopausal women.

A separate systematic review (Maturitas, 2011) of 4 RCTs in peri- and postmenopausal women found maca produced favorable changes on standardized menopausal symptom scales (Kupperman Index, Greene Climacteric Scale) compared with placebo.
cons: Both systematic reviews explicitly describe the evidence as limited -- small numbers of trials, small sample sizes, and methodological weaknesses (including inconsistent adverse-event reporting) mean firm conclusions cannot yet be drawn.

Maca has shown modest estrogenic activity in laboratory studies and has been associated with increased estradiol in at least one small trial in postmenopausal women; people with hormone-sensitive conditions (e.g., estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids) should use caution and consult a physician before use.
Direct clinical evidence for maca's effect on fatigue/energy specifically (as opposed to sexual function or menopausal symptoms) is thinner than for those other uses.

references.title: Maca (L. meyenii) for improving sexual function: a systematic review
Maca (Lepidium meyenii) for treatment of menopausal symptoms: A systematic review

en.history: Rhodiola rosea has a long history of traditional use in Scandinavian and Russian/Siberian folk medicine, where it was used to help withstand cold climates, increase physical endurance, and reduce fatigue -- classifying it as an adaptogen alongside herbs like ashwagandha and panax ginseng.
en.features: Rhodiola's proposed active compounds, salidroside and rosavins, are of ongoing research interest for modulating the stress response (including cortisol) and central neurotransmitter activity. Modern research has focused heavily on physical and mental fatigue, exercise performance, and stress-related symptoms, with generally positive but methodologically mixed results.
pros: A 2017 open-label multicenter trial (117 patients, 12 weeks, Neuropsychiatric Disease and Treatment) found a standardized 400 mg/day rhodiola extract was associated with substantial reductions in emotional exhaustion, fatigue, and perceived stress in patients with burnout symptoms, with good tolerability.

A phase III randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (Planta Medica, 2009; 60 participants) found the standardized SHR-5 extract improved fatigue level, attention, and cortisol response to awakening stress compared with placebo over a 4-week course.
cons: Evidence is genuinely mixed: a 2014 randomized controlled trial in nursing students on shift work (PLoS One, 42-day course) found rhodiola actually worsened fatigue scores compared with placebo, and a 2012 systematic review of 11 studies concluded that methodological flaws across the literature limit confidence in true efficacy.

Commercial rhodiola supplements vary widely in the concentration of active marker compounds (rosavins, salidroside) relative to what's stated on the label, so real-world product quality and dosing consistency is a genuine concern.

Because of its stimulating effects on noradrenergic, dopaminergic, and serotonergic activity, case reports describe rhodiola triggering manic episodes in people vulnerable to bipolar disorder; it is not recommended for people with bipolar disorder without medical supervision, and caution is also advised when combining with antidepressants or MAOIs.

references.title: Rhodiola Rosea for Mental and Physical Fatigue in Nursing Students: A Randomized Controlled Trial
Mania Associated With Rhodiola Rosea: An Adaptogen With Antidepressant Effects

en.history: Passiflora incarnata is native to the southeastern United States and was used by Indigenous peoples and later incorporated into 19th-century American herbal medicine as a calming remedy for nervousness and sleeplessness; it remains a common ingredient in European herbal sleep and anxiety preparations today.
en.features: Passionflower is thought to act on GABA receptors in the brain, which is consistent with its traditional use for calming and sedative effects. Modern clinical research has focused on generalized anxiety and sleep quality, often comparing it to standard anti-anxiety medications.
pros: A pilot double-blind randomized controlled trial (Journal of Clinical Pharmacy and Therapeutics, 2001; 36 outpatients with generalized anxiety disorder) found Passiflora extract as effective as oxazepam (30 mg/day) over 4 weeks, with significantly less job-performance impairment than the medication.

A double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study using polysomnography-validated sleep diaries found passionflower tea was associated with significantly better subjective sleep quality ratings than placebo over a week of use.

A 2020 systematic review (Nutrients) of 9 randomized clinical trials found the majority reported reduced anxiety with Passiflora preparations, with no serious adverse events, including no reported memory or cognitive impairment, across the reviewed trials.
cons: Most trials are small and short in duration, and some (including the oxazepam-comparison trial) are described by their own authors as pilot studies, so larger confirmatory trials are still needed.

Passionflower's GABA-modulating, sedative action can be additive with other sedating substances -- combining it with sedative or CNS-depressant medications (benzodiazepines, sleep medications, opioids, sedating antihistamines) may cause excess drowsiness, so this combination should be discussed with a physician.
Not well studied in pregnancy, and effects may be potentiated by alcohol.

references.title: Passionflower in the treatment of generalized anxiety: a pilot double-blind randomized controlled trial with oxazepam
Passiflora incarnata in Neuropsychiatric Disorders—A Systematic Review

en.history: Tanacetum parthenium has a long history in European folk medicine, traditionally taken for headaches, fevers, and menstrual complaints, with modern interest in migraine prevention emerging from British case reports in the 1970s.
en.features: Contains parthenolide, a sesquiterpene lactone thought to influence serotonin release and platelet activity. Modern research has focused almost entirely on migraine frequency rather than acute migraine relief.
pros: A 2015 Cochrane systematic review of 6 randomized, double-blind trials (561 participants) found the largest, most recent trial (n=218) showed feverfew reduced migraine frequency by about 0.6 attacks per month more than placebo (from a baseline of roughly 4.8 attacks/month).
Across the reviewed trials, adverse events were generally mild and transient, most commonly gastrointestinal complaints and mouth ulcers, with no major safety signals identified.
cons: The Cochrane reviewers graded the evidence as low quality, with mixed results across trials (some smaller trials positive, larger rigorous trials showing no significant benefit for symptom intensity, duration, or associated nausea) -- confirmation in larger, high-quality trials is still needed.

May inhibit platelet aggregation and reduce thromboxane production; because of a theoretical additive bleeding risk, it is generally advised to avoid feverfew when taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet medication.
Not recommended during pregnancy, as it has traditionally been used to stimulate uterine contractions.

references.title: Feverfew for preventing migraine (Cochrane systematic review)
Feverfew for preventing migraine — Cochrane Library summary

en.history: Viburnum opulus bark has a long tradition in Western and Eastern European folk herbalism as an antispasmodic, historically used for muscle cramps and menstrual pain, with the common name "cramp bark" directly reflecting this traditional use.
en.features: Contains coumarins (notably scopoletin) and the compound viopudial, both studied for smooth-muscle relaxant activity. Most research to date is preclinical -- in vitro and animal tissue studies -- rather than human clinical trials.
pros: In vitro studies on isolated uterine tissue show scopoletin acts as a calcium-channel antagonist and viopudial produces measurable spasmolytic (muscle-relaxing) activity, providing a plausible mechanism for the herb's traditional antispasmodic use.

Comparative animal-tissue research found Viburnum opulus extract roughly four times more active than the related species Viburnum prunifolium at relaxing rat uterine contractions, consistent with its traditional reputation as a stronger antispasmodic.
cons: No randomized controlled human trials evaluating cramp bark for menstrual cramping or muscle tension were found -- current support rests on in vitro/animal pharmacology and long-standing traditional use, not clinical outcome data, consistent with its classification here as in-vitro/cellular-level evidence.
Documented adverse effects include dyspeptic complaints and abdominal pain, with at least one reported case of acute pancreatitis linked to concentrated extract use.
Not recommended during pregnancy given its traditional use as a uterine-active herb, even though direct pregnancy safety data is limited.

references.title: Viburnum opulus L. — A Review of Phytochemistry and Biological Effects
Viburnum opulus — Medicinal Plant Monograph, Southern Cross University

en.history: Vitex agnus-castus (chaste tree) fruit has been used since antiquity -- referenced by Hippocrates and Dioscorides -- traditionally associated with women's reproductive health and, per its common name, historically believed to reduce libido in monastic settings.
en.features: Its extracts act on dopamine D2 receptors, which is thought to modestly lower prolactin levels, and contain mild phytoestrogenic flavonoids such as apigenin. Modern research has concentrated on premenstrual syndrome (PMS/PMDD) and cyclical breast pain.
pros: A 2017 systematic review and meta-analysis (Verkaik et al., American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology) pooling double-blind, placebo-controlled trials found a large pooled effect on total PMS symptoms (Hedges' g -1.21), with 13 of 14 placebo/comparator trials reporting positive effects.

An 8-cycle prospective multicenter trial of a standardized 20 mg extract (Ze 440) in 43 women found a 42.5% reduction in PMS symptom scores, with 20 of 43 participants classified as treatment responders (at least 50% symptom reduction).
cons: The 2017 meta-analysis itself cautions that extremely high heterogeneity (I2 91%) and high risk of bias across included trials mean the pooled effect likely overestimates the true benefit -- larger, higher-quality trials are still needed for a definitive conclusion.

Because of its dopaminergic and mild estrogenic activity, it may interact with hormonal medications (including oral contraceptives) and dopamine agonists/antagonists, and is generally advised against for anyone with a hormone-sensitive condition such as estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer.
Not recommended during pregnancy given its hormonal activity.

references.title: The treatment of premenstrual syndrome with preparations of Vitex agnus castus: a systematic review and meta-analysis
Efficacy of Vitex agnus castus L. extract Ze 440 in patients with pre-menstrual syndrome (PMS)

en.history: Psyllium husk, from the seeds of Plantago ovata, has been used for centuries in South Asian and Middle Eastern traditional medicine as a bulking laxative, and is one of the most widely studied dietary fibers in modern gastroenterology.
en.features: A soluble, mucilaginous, largely non-fermentable fiber that absorbs water in the gut to form a gel, increasing stool bulk and softness. Modern research covers constipation relief as well as cardiovascular and glycemic effects.
pros: A 2022 systematic review and meta-analysis in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (16 randomized controlled trials, 1,251 participants) found 66% of fiber-treated participants responded versus 41% on control, with psyllium showing the strongest and most consistent effect among the fibers studied (increasing stool frequency by roughly 3 bowel movements per week).
The same review found psyllium also improved stool consistency and reduced straining severity, with benefits most apparent at doses above 10 g/day taken for at least 4 weeks.
cons: Fiber supplementation, including psyllium, significantly increased flatulence severity compared to control in the pooled analysis -- a common reason for discontinuation.
Must be taken with adequate water; taking psyllium without enough fluid can worsen bloating or, rarely, cause choking or esophageal/bowel obstruction.
Can reduce absorption of some oral medications if taken at the same time, so it is generally advised to separate dosing by a couple of hours.

references.title: The Effect of Fiber Supplementation on Chronic Constipation in Adults: An Updated Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials
The Effect of Fiber Supplementation on Chronic Constipation in Adults (full text, PMC)

en.history: Boswellia serrata resin (Indian frankincense) has been used for thousands of years in Ayurvedic medicine as an anti-inflammatory agent for joint and musculoskeletal complaints, and remains one of the most extensively clinically studied Ayurvedic botanicals for osteoarthritis.
en.features: Contains boswellic acids, particularly AKBA (acetyl-11-keto-beta-boswellic acid), studied for inhibiting 5-lipoxygenase and reducing pro-inflammatory leukotriene production. Most modern clinical research has focused specifically on knee osteoarthritis pain and function.
pros: A 2020 systematic review and meta-analysis (BMC Complementary Medicine and Therapies, 7 RCTs, 545 patients) found statistically significant improvements in pain (WOMAC pain WMD -14.22, P=0.0006), stiffness (WOMAC stiffness WMD -10.04, P=0.0007), and function (WOMAC function WMD -10.75, P<0.00001) versus control.

A separate large network meta-analysis of 39 randomized controlled trials confirmed Boswellia significantly improved pain, stiffness, and function in knee osteoarthritis, with specific extracts like 5-Loxin showing large effect sizes for short-term pain reduction.
Across trials, adverse event rates were not significantly different between Boswellia and control groups, suggesting general short-term tolerability.
cons: The 2020 meta-analysis itself flags high heterogeneity across outcomes (I2 47-99%) and medium-to-low quality of the underlying RCTs, meaning results should be interpreted cautiously pending larger, more rigorous trials.
Most trials are short-term (typically 4-12 weeks), so long-term safety and efficacy data beyond that window is limited.
Not recommended during pregnancy, as safety data in pregnant populations is lacking and some traditional sources note uterine-stimulant activity.

references.title: Effectiveness of Boswellia and Boswellia extract for osteoarthritis patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis
Effectiveness of Boswellia and Boswellia extract for osteoarthritis patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis (journal page)

en.history: Bacopa monnieri (Brahmi) has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for over 1,000 years, referenced in classical texts such as the Charaka Samhita as a "medhya rasayana" (mind-rejuvenating herb) for supporting memory, learning, and mental clarity.
en.features: Its primary active constituents are triterpenoid saponins called bacosides, which cross the blood-brain barrier and are studied for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and mild acetylcholinesterase-inhibiting activity -- supporting acetylcholine levels involved in memory consolidation. Most modern research has focused on cognitive performance (attention, memory recall) in healthy adults and older populations.
pros: A 2014 meta-analysis of 9 randomized controlled trials (518 subjects) published in the Journal of Ethnopharmacology found Bacopa monnieri extract improved cognitive performance, particularly speed of attention, compared to placebo.

A double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in adults with age-associated memory impairment (125 mg twice daily for 12 weeks) found significant improvement in mental control, logical memory, and paired-associate learning versus placebo.
Traditional use for anxiety is echoed in some modern trials reporting reduced anxiety alongside cognitive gains, though anxiety was a secondary rather than primary endpoint in most studies.
cons: Animal research suggests Bacopa may raise thyroxine (T4) levels; caution is advised for anyone with a thyroid condition or taking thyroid medication, since combined use could push thyroid hormone levels too high.
Not recommended during pregnancy due to insufficient human safety data.
Effects typically take 4-12 weeks of consistent use to appear, and gastrointestinal side effects (nausea, cramping, dry mouth) are commonly reported at higher doses.

references.title: Meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials on cognitive effects of Bacopa monnieri extract
Randomized controlled trial of standardized Bacopa monniera extract in age-associated memory impairment

en.history: Tinospora cordifolia (Guduchi, also known as Giloy) is a climbing shrub used for centuries in Ayurveda as a "rasayana" tonic, traditionally taken to support general vitality, digestion, and the body's resistance to illness.
en.features: Contains alkaloids, diterpenoid lactones, and polysaccharides studied for immunomodulatory and antioxidant activity. Modern research attention increased sharply during the COVID-19 pandemic, when Guduchi was widely used in India as a traditional immune-support supplement, prompting both efficacy studies and closer scrutiny of its safety profile.
pros: A randomized, open-label, three-arm trial (136 completing participants) published in the European Journal of Medical Research found Tinospora cordifolia extract (500 mg), alone or combined with Adhatoda vasica, was associated with faster viral clearance and reduced inflammatory markers in mild COVID-19 compared to standard care, with no serious adverse effects reported.

Traditional use for general wellness and mild upper-respiratory discomfort is longstanding in Ayurvedic practice, though rigorous clinical trials isolating Guduchi's effect on symptoms like sore throat specifically remain limited.
cons: Real, documented case reports link Tinospora cordifolia to autoimmune-type liver injury: a 2021 case series in the Journal of Clinical and Experimental Hepatology described six patients who developed acute hepatitis (several with autoimmune features, some with pre-existing silent liver disease unmasked by use) after a median of 90 days of use, with most recovering only after stopping the herb and, in several cases, steroid treatment.

Because it stimulates immune activity, Guduchi may worsen symptoms in autoimmune conditions such as lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, or multiple sclerosis, and is not recommended for people with autoimmune disease.

Given the hepatotoxicity case reports, anyone with existing liver disease should avoid Guduchi, and any unexplained fatigue, jaundice, or abdominal discomfort during use warrants stopping and seeking medical evaluation.

references.title: Adhatoda vasica and Tinospora cordifolia extracts ameliorate clinical and molecular markers in mild COVID-19 patients: a randomized open-label three-armed study
Herbal Immune Booster-Induced Liver Injury in the COVID-19 Pandemic - A Case Series

en.history: Phyllanthus emblica (Amla, Indian gooseberry) is one of the most widely used fruits in Ayurvedic medicine, prized for millennia as a core ingredient of rejuvenating formulations such as Chyawanprash and valued traditionally for its very high vitamin C and antioxidant content.
en.features: Rich in vitamin C, gallic acid, and other polyphenols studied for antioxidant activity; modern research has focused particularly on cardiometabolic markers such as lipid profile, oxidative stress, and endothelial (blood vessel lining) function.
pros: A 2023 systematic review and meta-analysis of 5 randomized controlled trials (published in Diabetes & Metabolic Syndrome) found Amla supplementation was associated with significant reductions in LDL cholesterol, total cholesterol, triglycerides, fasting glucose, and C-reactive protein, alongside an increase in HDL cholesterol.

A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in adults with metabolic syndrome (59 completers, 500 mg twice daily for 12 weeks) found significant improvements in endothelial function, oxidative stress markers, and lipid profile compared to placebo, with no serious adverse events.
cons: Amla has measurable antiplatelet activity in the research literature, which may increase bleeding risk when combined with blood-thinning medications (e.g., warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel); use with caution alongside anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy and before scheduled surgery.
Because it can lower blood glucose, combining Amla with diabetes medication may increase the risk of hypoglycemia and blood sugar should be monitored.

High doses (commonly cited above roughly 5 g/day) are associated with gastrointestinal upset, including heartburn, cramping, or diarrhea; most supportive trials used doses well below this and were relatively short (3-12 weeks), so long-term safety data is limited.

references.title: The impact of Emblica Officinalis (Amla) on lipid profile, glucose, and C-reactive protein: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials

Evaluation of the effects of a standardized aqueous extract of Phyllanthus emblica fruits on endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, systemic inflammation and lipid profile in subjects with metabolic syndrome: a randomised, double blind, placebo controlled clinical study

en.history: Azadirachta indica (Neem) has been used topically in Ayurvedic and traditional Indian skincare for centuries, valued for its bitter, astringent properties and long applied to support clear, healthy-looking skin.
en.features: Neem leaf and seed extracts contain nimbin, nimbidin, and azadirachtin among other compounds studied for antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity against skin bacteria implicated in acne and irritation. This entry is scoped specifically to topical use -- internal, high-dose use of neem is a separate matter with its own distinct and more serious safety profile (see below).
pros: A systematic review of randomized controlled trials on neem for acne identified 2 single-blind trials conducted in India (121 total patients, 6-week duration) reporting positive subjective and objective improvement in acne with non-serious adverse events, though the authors noted the evidence base is still small.

A published open-label clinical study (120 adults, 4 weeks) of a neem-and-turmeric face wash found statistically significant reductions in sebum levels and increases in skin hydration (p < 0.001), along with reduction or no new appearance of acne lesions in the majority of participants; note this trial combined neem with turmeric and was not placebo-controlled.
cons: Internal or high-dose use of neem (seed oil especially) is a separate and much more serious matter from topical use: documented case reports describe severe poisoning in children, including metabolic acidosis, seizures, liver and kidney injury, and some fatalities, after oral ingestion of neem oil -- oral neem should never be given to children.

Animal studies show reversible reproductive toxicity with internal/chronic neem exposure (disrupted estrus cycle and higher pregnancy loss in females, reduced sperm count and motility in males); neem is not recommended internally during pregnancy or for pediatric use, and this entry's evidence and use case is limited to topical skin application only.

The topical acne evidence base itself remains limited to a small number of short, mostly single-blind or open-label trials -- larger, double-blind, longer-duration studies are still needed before efficacy claims can be considered well established.

references.title: Clinical study to assess efficacy and safety of Purifying Neem Face Wash in prevention and reduction of acne in healthy adults
Azadirachta indica (Neem) as a Potential Natural Active for Dermocosmetic and Topical Products: A Narrative Review

en.history: Trigonella foenum-graecum (fenugreek) seeds have been used for thousands of years across Ayurvedic, Middle Eastern, and Mediterranean traditional medicine, both as a culinary spice and for digestive and metabolic support.
en.features: Fenugreek seeds are rich in soluble fiber (galactomannan), saponins, and 4-hydroxyisoleucine, compounds studied for slowing carbohydrate absorption and supporting insulin sensitivity. Most modern clinical research has focused on blood glucose and lipid parameters in people with, or at risk of, type 2 diabetes.
pros: A 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis of 26 randomized controlled trials (Avicenna Journal of Phytomedicine) found fenugreek seed supplementation significantly reduced fasting blood glucose, 2-hour postprandial glucose, HbA1c, and insulin levels compared to control.

A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in type 2 diabetics (81 completers, 500 mg twice daily for 12 weeks, added on to existing metformin/sulfonylurea therapy) found significantly greater reductions in fasting glucose, postprandial glucose, and HbA1c versus placebo, with no adverse events reported.
cons: Not recommended in pregnancy at supplement/concentrated doses: fenugreek has documented uterine-stimulant effects in traditional and animal research and has been linked to increased risk of early contractions; ordinary culinary/food-level amounts are viewed differently than concentrated supplements, but supplement-dose use should be avoided during pregnancy.
Because it lowers blood glucose, combining fenugreek with diabetes medication increases the risk of hypoglycemia and requires blood sugar monitoring, especially when starting or changing doses.

Fenugreek may have mild blood-thinning activity and could add to the effect of anticoagulant medication; most supportive trials were also relatively short (weeks to a few months), so long-term safety data is limited, and gastrointestinal effects (bloating, diarrhea) are commonly reported at higher doses.

references.title: The effects of fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum) seed on glycemic parameters: An updated systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
A randomized double blind placebo controlled trial to assess the safety and efficacy of a patented fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum) seed extract in Type 2 diabetics

en.history: Centella asiatica, known in Ayurveda as "mandukaparni," has been used for centuries in traditional Indian and Southeast Asian medicine for wound care and skin conditions, and is also a long-standing herb in traditional Chinese medicine.
en.features: Its key constituents are pentacyclic triterpenoids (asiaticoside, madecassoside, asiatic acid), which are thought to stimulate collagen synthesis and reduce inflammation. Modern research has focused mainly on topical/oral wound healing and, separately, on cognitive and mood-related outcomes.
pros: A 2022 systematic review of 4 randomized controlled trials found favorable effects on wound healing, including faster healing time in burn wound patients (14.67 days vs. 21.53 days with standard treatment) and improved wound contraction and granulation.

A systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (693 participants) found Centella asiatica improved mood measures -- increased alertness (SMD 0.71) and decreased anger scores (SMD -0.81) -- and that a single 750 mg dose improved working memory speed in healthy elderly adults, though no significant effect was found across broader cognitive domains overall.
cons: Rare but documented case reports of liver injury (including jaundice) exist with oral use; caution is advised for anyone with pre-existing liver disease.
Not recommended during pregnancy -- there is not enough reliable safety data on oral use.

The same meta-analysis that found mood benefits found no significant improvement across formal cognitive-function domains (attention, memory, executive function) compared to placebo, so cognitive support evidence is milder than the skin-healing evidence.

references.title: A Systematic Review of the Effect of Centella asiatica on Wound Healing
Effects of Centella asiatica (L.) Urb. on cognitive function and mood related outcomes: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis

en.history: Asparagus racemosus, known as "shatavari" in Ayurveda, has classically been used as a "rasayana" (rejuvenative) herb for women's reproductive health, including support during lactation and the menopausal transition.
en.features: Its root contains steroidal saponins (shatavarins) with phytoestrogenic and adaptogenic properties. Modern research has focused on menopausal symptom relief, hormonal markers, and lactation support.
pros: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 50 perimenopausal women (120 days, standardized extract 200 mg/day) found significant reductions in Menopausal Rating Scale scores and hot flash frequency versus placebo (p < 0.001), along with reductions in FSH and LH.

A double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial in 60 lactating mothers (60 mg/kg/day for 30 days) found more than a three-fold greater increase in prolactin levels and significantly greater infant weight gain compared to placebo.
cons: Shatavari's phytoestrogenic activity means it should be used cautiously, if at all, in people with hormone-sensitive conditions (e.g., certain breast, uterine, or ovarian conditions) without medical guidance.
Positive trials used specific standardized proprietary extracts (e.g., CL22205) at defined doses -- results may not generalize to unstandardized raw shatavari preparations.
Most clinical trials are short-to-moderate in duration (weeks to a few months), so long-term safety data is limited.

references.title: A Standardized Asparagus Racemosus Root Extract Improves Hormonal Balance and Menstrual Health and Reduces Vasomotor Symptoms in Perimenopausal Women: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Study
A Double-Blind Randomized Clinical Trial for Evaluation of Galactogogue Activity of Asparagus racemosus Willd

en.history: Commiphora wightii resin ("guggul") has been used in Ayurvedic medicine since antiquity, described in classical texts such as the Sushruta Samhita for lipid disorders ("medoroga") and joint complaints.
en.features: Its active compounds, guggulsterones, are proposed to act on lipid-regulating pathways and to have anti-inflammatory effects. Modern research has focused mainly on cholesterol management and joint/osteoarthritis symptoms, with genuinely inconsistent results across trials.
pros: A quasi-experimental outcomes study in 30 patients with knee osteoarthritis found significant improvement in WOMAC scores (p < 0.0001) after one month, with continued improvement at two months and no reported side effects.

A small Norwegian randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (34 completers, 12 weeks) found significant reductions in total cholesterol and HDL cholesterol with guggul versus placebo, though the authors themselves noted the clinical significance of the change was unclear.
cons: Evidence for cholesterol benefit is genuinely mixed: a larger, more rigorous JAMA-published randomized controlled trial (guggulipid vs. placebo, 8 weeks) found guggulipid actually raised LDL cholesterol by 4-5% versus placebo, with no significant benefit on total cholesterol, HDL, or triglycerides -- the opposite of the effect it's traditionally used for.
May increase thyroid hormone activity and interact with thyroid medication -- not recommended for anyone with a thyroid condition without medical supervision.

Guggulsterones show estrogen- and progesterone-receptor activity in preliminary research, so caution is advised for hormone-sensitive conditions and during pregnancy; guggul may also have antiplatelet activity, raising a theoretical bleeding-risk interaction with anticoagulant medications.

references.title: Guggulipid for the Treatment of Hypercholesterolemia: A Randomized Controlled Trial
The effectiveness of Commiphora mukul for osteoarthritis of the knee: an outcomes study

en.history: Terminalia arjuna bark has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for centuries as a cardiotonic, described in classical texts for supporting "hridroga" (heart-related conditions).
en.features: Its bark contains cardioactive glycosides and antioxidant compounds (including arjunolic acid) studied for possible effects on heart muscle function and blood vessel health. Modern research has focused on heart failure, angina, and general cardiovascular support.
pros: An early double-blind crossover trial in 12 patients with severe refractory heart failure (NYHA Class IV) found improvement in NYHA functional class and significant increases in left ventricular ejection fraction versus placebo (p < 0.005), sustained over long-term open-label follow-up.

A more recent double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial in 100 chronic heart failure patients (750 mg twice daily, 12 weeks, added to standard therapy) found improvements in functional capacity (6-minute walk distance) and quality-of-life measures, and the extract was well tolerated.
cons: That same 100-patient 2016 trial found no significant change in the primary outcome, left ventricular ejection fraction, versus placebo -- so more rigorous testing hasn't reproduced the dramatic effect seen in the earlier, much smaller trial.
Terminalia arjuna shows antiplatelet and anticoagulant-like activity in laboratory studies, raising a theoretical bleeding-risk interaction with anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications.
Researchers note a lack of standardization and bioavailability data across studies, and formal drug-interaction studies (e.g., with statins or blood pressure medication) are still lacking.

references.title: Salutary effect of Terminalia Arjuna in patients with severe refractory heart failure
Clinical efficacy of water extract of stem bark of Terminalia arjuna (Roxb. ex DC.) Wight & Arn. in patients of chronic heart failure: a double-blind, randomized controlled trial

en.history: Boerhavia diffusa, called "punarnava" (meaning roughly "renewer") in Ayurveda, has traditionally been used to support healthy fluid balance and kidney/liver function.
en.features: It contains alkaloids (notably punarnavine) and flavonoids credited with diuretic and anti-inflammatory activity. Most of the pharmacological evidence to date comes from animal and cell-based studies rather than large modern human trials.
pros: A 2023 ethnomedicinal review summarizing the available evidence describes documented diuretic and renoprotective traditional use, and cites a small human clinical study in diabetic nephropathy patients in which six months of Boerhavia diffusa treatment was associated with a decrease in 24-hour urine protein excretion (though the change did not reach statistical significance).

Cell-based research on Boerhavia diffusa root fractions has shown protective effects against oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage in kidney cell lines, offering a plausible mechanism consistent with its traditional use for fluid balance and kidney support.
cons: Direct human clinical evidence remains genuinely limited -- available human trials are small, older, and often methodologically weak (e.g., a decades-old 22-patient nephrotic syndrome trial with substantial drop-out), and most of the mechanistic support still comes from animal or in vitro models rather than rigorous modern RCTs.
Not recommended during pregnancy -- safety data is insufficient.

Given its active diuretic effect on the kidneys, anyone with existing kidney disease should use it only under medical supervision rather than self-directed, since its effects on already-compromised kidney function haven't been well characterized in controlled human trials.

references.title: Ethnomedicinal values of Boerhaavia diffusa L. as a panacea against multiple human ailments: a state of art review
Attenuation of tacrolimus induced oxidative stress, mitochondrial damage, and cell cycle arrest by Boerhavia diffusa root fraction in mdck cell lines

en.history: Astragalus membranaceus (Huang Qi) has been used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for centuries as one of the classic "Qi tonic" herbs, traditionally taken to build stamina and support recovery from illness or fatigue.
en.features: Contains polysaccharides, saponins (astragalosides), and flavonoids thought to underlie its immunomodulatory effects. Modern research has focused mainly on its role as an adjunct for cancer-related fatigue and, more broadly, on general immune support.
pros: A 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis of 8 randomized controlled trials (Integrative Cancer Therapies) found Astragalus membranaceus significantly reduced cancer-related fatigue (SMD = -1.63, 95% CI [-1.90, -1.36]) and improved quality of life (SMD = 0.86) versus control, though the authors noted the underlying trials were small and of variable quality.

A double-blind, randomized, controlled preliminary study in 64 poststroke patients (Journal of Ethnopharmacology, 2016) found 28 days of Astragalus membranaceus significantly improved Brief Fatigue Index scores and global quality-of-life measures compared to control.
cons: Because of its immune-stimulating action, it is generally advised against for people with autoimmune conditions or those on immunosuppressive therapy, since it could theoretically aggravate immune overactivity -- direct clinical evidence in autoimmune populations is limited.

Systematic reviews of Astragalus for preventing upper respiratory infections found no high-quality evidence of efficacy, reflecting a broader pattern of small sample sizes and low methodological quality across the evidence base.
Not recommended during pregnancy; safety data in pregnant populations is lacking.

references.title: Efficacy of Astragalus Membranaceus (Huang Qi) for Cancer-Related Fatigue: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Studies
Effects of the traditional Chinese herb Astragalus membranaceus in patients with poststroke fatigue: A double-blind, randomized, controlled preliminary study

en.history: Panax ginseng has been used for roughly 2,000 years in traditional Chinese and Korean medicine as a foundational "Qi tonic," taken to support energy, vitality, and resilience to physical and mental stress.
en.features: Its ginsenosides are classified as adaptogenic compounds. Modern research has concentrated on fatigue (particularly disease-related fatigue) and cognitive performance under stress, with mixed results depending on the population studied.
pros: A 2016 meta-analysis of 12 randomized controlled trials (630 participants, Journal of Korean Medical Science) found ginseng supplementation produced a statistically significant reduction in fatigue (SMD = 0.34) versus placebo, though the authors cautioned the evidence base still has few, small trials and found no significant effect on physical performance.

A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial in 50 healthy adults (Mariage et al., Pharmaceuticals, 2020) found a standardized Panax ginseng preparation (HRG80) significantly improved attention and memory and reduced perceived stress during stressful cognitive tasks compared with placebo.
cons: Not all trials are positive: a larger double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 127 patients with advanced cancer (Yennurajalingam et al., JNCCN, 2017) found Panax ginseng was not significantly better than placebo for cancer-related fatigue after 4 weeks of treatment.

A 2010 Cochrane systematic review of 9 RCTs concluded there was a lack of convincing evidence for a cognitive-enhancing effect of ginseng in healthy adults, and no high-quality evidence in dementia patients.

May raise blood pressure, so caution (and a contraindication) applies for people with hypertension; it may also interact with anticoagulant/antiplatelet medications such as warfarin, with mixed evidence on bleeding risk -- caution is advised for anyone on blood thinners.

references.title: Efficacy of Ginseng Supplements on Fatigue and Physical Performance: a Meta-analysis

Efficacy of Panax ginseng Meyer Herbal Preparation HRG80 in Preventing and Mitigating Stress-Induced Failure of Cognitive Functions in Healthy Subjects: A Pilot, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Crossover Trial

en.history: Schisandra chinensis (Wu Wei Zi, "five-flavor fruit") has been used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for over 2,000 years as a Qi and Jing tonic, traditionally taken to support endurance, vitality, and resilience to stress.
en.features: Contains lignans (schisandrin, schisandrol, gomisins) believed to underlie its adaptogenic and hepatoprotective properties. Modern research has focused on physical fatigue/endurance and, separately, on liver-supportive effects, though most of the mechanistic liver research remains preclinical.
pros: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 45 postmenopausal women (Park et al., International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 2020) found 12 weeks of Schisandra chinensis extract (1000 mg/day) significantly increased quadriceps muscle strength and decreased resting blood lactate compared to placebo.

A widely cited review of clinical evidence for adaptogens (Panossian & Wikman, Current Clinical Pharmacology, 2009) rated the evidence for Schisandra chinensis increasing endurance and mental performance in patients with mild fatigue as "good."
cons: Human evidence for liver-supportive effects comes mainly from small, lower-quality trials in hepatitis or fatty-liver-disease patients; the more rigorous mechanistic hepatoprotective research is still preclinical (animal models), so this benefit should be considered preliminary.

Traditionally avoided during pregnancy because of documented uterine-stimulant (oxytocic) effects -- historically it was used specifically to aid labor, which is why it carries a pregnancy contraindication here.
May worsen GERD or peptic ulcer symptoms by increasing stomach acid, and may potentiate certain medications (e.g., barbiturates); anyone on other medications should check for interactions before use.

references.title: Effect of Schisandra Chinensis Extract Supplementation on Quadriceps Muscle Strength and Fatigue in Adult Women: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial
Evidence-based efficacy of adaptogens in fatigue, and molecular mechanisms related to their stress-protective activity

en.history: Ganoderma lucidum (Lingzhi/Reishi) has been used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for over 2,000 years, historically regarded as a symbol of longevity and used as a general tonic for vitality and calm.
en.features: Contains triterpenoids (ganoderic acids) and polysaccharides (beta-glucans) studied for immune-modulating effects. Modern human trials have focused mostly on fatigue and general well-being; direct human sleep-specific trial evidence is comparatively limited, with more of the sleep-related support coming from traditional use and animal/mechanistic studies.
pros: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 132 patients with neurasthenia (Tang et al., Journal of Medical Food, 2005) found 8 weeks of a standardized Ganoderma lucidum polysaccharide extract significantly reduced fatigue and improved global clinical impression scores compared to placebo.

A pilot randomized controlled trial in 48 breast cancer patients (Zhao et al., Evidence-Based Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2012) found Ganoderma lucidum spore powder significantly improved fatigue and physical well-being subscale scores, with reductions in inflammatory markers (TNF-alpha, IL-6), versus placebo.
cons: Direct human randomized-trial evidence for insomnia/sleep specifically is limited -- much of the sleep-related rationale currently rests on traditional use and animal/mechanistic studies rather than large controlled human sleep trials.

Reishi has documented antiplatelet and anticoagulant activity (inhibits platelet aggregation, prolongs coagulation), and case reports describe elevated INR and bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulants such as warfarin -- caution is strongly advised for anyone on blood-thinning medication.

Evidence is mixed across conditions: a placebo-controlled pilot trial in women with fibromyalgia found no statistically significant difference in mood or quality-of-life outcomes versus placebo, only a non-significant favorable trend.

references.title: A randomized, double-blind and placebo-controlled study of a Ganoderma lucidum polysaccharide extract in neurasthenia
Spore Powder of Ganoderma lucidum Improves Cancer-Related Fatigue in Breast Cancer Patients Undergoing Endocrine Therapy: A Pilot Clinical Trial

en.history: Polygonum multiflorum (He Shou Wu / Fo-Ti) has been used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for centuries, particularly as a Liver/Kidney tonic traditionally taken to "blacken hair" and address premature graying and hair loss.
en.features: Contains stilbene glycosides (notably TSG) and anthraquinones (including emodin). Modern research has focused heavily on documenting and understanding a serious, immune-mediated liver-injury risk associated with this herb, alongside preclinical animal studies of hair-pigmentation mechanisms -- human clinical-trial evidence for hair or anti-aging benefits is traditional/anecdotal, not established through controlled human trials.
pros: An animal-model mechanistic study (Han et al., BioMed Research International, 2015) found raw Polygonum multiflorum root extract increased melanin content and reduced hair graying in a mouse model, identifying candidate pathways (alpha-MSH, MC1R, tyrosinase) -- this supports the plausibility of the traditional use, but it is preclinical (animal) evidence, not a human clinical trial.

Its use as a Liver/Kidney tonic for hair and aging is long-standing and consistent across traditional Chinese medicine sources, but this reflects historical/traditional use rather than evidence from controlled human efficacy trials.
cons: Real, well-documented risk of serious liver injury: Polygonum multiflorum is one of the herbal products most frequently implicated in clinically apparent acute liver injury (hepatocellular and cholestatic patterns), and per NIH LiverTox, up to approximately 10% of clinically apparent cases have been fatal or required urgent liver transplantation. This is not a low-risk herb, and anyone considering it should be aware of this real safety risk before use.

The injury appears to be immune-mediated: carriage of the HLA-B*35:01 allele is strongly associated with susceptibility to this liver injury, meaning risk may be elevated and less predictable for people with an underlying autoimmune tendency -- consistent with the caution here for anyone with an autoimmune condition, and this herb should be avoided entirely by anyone with existing liver disease.

Because efficacy evidence for hair and aging benefits is traditional/anecdotal only, with no robust human clinical trials, the documented hepatotoxicity risk is not offset by strong proof of benefit -- this combination makes it a meaningfully higher-risk herb relative to others with better-established safety and evidence profiles.

references.title: Polygonum Multiflorum - LiverTox: Clinical and Research Information on Drug-Induced Liver Injury
The hepatotoxicity of Polygonum multiflorum: The emerging role of the immune-mediated liver injury

en.history: Angelica sinensis, known as dong quai, has been used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for over a thousand years, traditionally called "female ginseng" and included in classical multi-herb formulas such as Si Wu Tang for women's reproductive health and blood circulation.
en.features: Contains coumarin derivatives (including ligustilide and ferulic acid) studied for mild antispasmodic and vasodilatory activity. Almost all of the clinical research showing benefit has tested dong quai as one ingredient within multi-herb TCM combination formulas, not as a standalone extract.
pros: Randomized, double-blind trials of multi-herb combination formulas containing dong quai (paired with herbs such as black cohosh and soy isoflavones) have reported reductions in menopausal vasomotor symptoms and menstrual migraine frequency.

Traditional use and laboratory research suggest ligustilide and related constituents have mild antispasmodic activity potentially relevant to menstrual cramping, though this has not been confirmed in dedicated standalone human trials.
cons: The most rigorous standalone trial to date -- a 1997 double-blind, placebo-controlled study of 71 postmenopausal women given dong quai alone for 24 weeks (Hirata et al., Fertility and Sterility) -- found no significant difference from placebo in hot flash frequency and no estrogen-like effect on vaginal or endometrial tissue.

Traditional Chinese medicine practitioners themselves rarely prescribe dong quai alone; positive results are concentrated in combination-formula trials, so evidence for the standalone herb specifically is genuinely weak.

Contains coumarin-type compounds that can potentiate anticoagulant medication -- published case reports describe elevated INR and increased bleeding risk in patients on warfarin. Not recommended during pregnancy, and caution is advised for anyone with a hormone-sensitive condition given its traditionally described estrogenic-adjacent activity.

references.title: Does dong quai have estrogenic effects in postmenopausal women? A double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
