en.history: Lycium barbarum fruit (goji berry, gou qi zi) has been used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for centuries as a tonic food traditionally believed to nourish the liver and kidney and support overall vitality, most often consumed as a food ingredient in soups and teas rather than a standalone extract.
en.features: Rich in carotenoids (notably zeaxanthin), polysaccharides, and vitamin C. Modern human research has focused mainly on antioxidant biomarkers and general well-being or fatigue rather than disease-specific outcomes.
pros: A 30-day randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 50 healthy adults aged 55-72 found that goji juice (120 mL/day) significantly increased antioxidant enzyme markers (superoxide dismutase +8.4%, glutathione peroxidase +9.9%) and decreased the oxidative stress marker malondialdehyde (-8.7%) compared with placebo.

Small human trials report improvements in self-reported fatigue, sleep quality, and general well-being with daily goji juice consumption, alongside a favorable short-term safety profile in the same studies.
cons: Most human evidence comes from small or observational studies rather than large, long-duration randomized trials, so reported benefits for fatigue and general well-being should be considered preliminary.

Goji berries can inhibit the CYP2C9 enzyme that metabolizes warfarin -- multiple published case reports describe markedly elevated INR and bleeding events (including nosebleeds, bruising, and rectal bleeding) in patients who combined goji juice or wine with warfarin. Anyone on anticoagulant medication should use caution and discuss increased INR monitoring with their prescriber.

en.history: Cordyceps sinensis (dong chong xia cao) has a centuries-long history in Traditional Chinese and Tibetan medicine, traditionally used as a tonic to support lung and kidney function, stamina, and recovery from illness.
en.features: Modern clinical research largely uses Cs-4, a lab-fermented mycelial strain developed to reflect the active constituents (such as cordycepin and polysaccharides) of the rare wild fungus, studied mainly for aerobic exercise capacity, respiratory symptom support, and immune modulation.
pros: A 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis of 14 randomized controlled trials (528 athletes) found that Cordyceps sinensis supplementation at 2-3 g/day for 6-12 weeks produced statistically significant improvements in endurance performance, ventilatory threshold, and VO2peak compared with control.

A systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 interventional studies (1,238 participants) found oral Cordyceps sinensis preparations were associated with improvements in lung function, exercise endurance, and quality of life in patients with stable COPD, with no serious adverse events reported -- though the authors noted no included trials were placebo-controlled or of high methodological quality.
cons: Because of its immune-stimulating polysaccharide content, Cordyceps may aggravate autoimmune conditions (such as lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, or multiple sclerosis) and could theoretically interfere with immunosuppressant medication -- caution is warranted for anyone with an autoimmune condition.
Safety during pregnancy has not been adequately studied in humans, so it is generally not recommended.

Much of the strongest trial evidence involves lab-cultivated Cs-4 mycelium rather than wild-harvested Cordyceps sinensis, and the COPD evidence base specifically lacks placebo-controlled, high-quality trials, so independent long-term safety and efficacy data remain limited.

references.title: Effectiveness and Safety of Oral Cordyceps sinensis on Stable COPD of GOLD Stages 2-3: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
Effects of fungal supplementation on endurance, immune function, and hematological profiles in adult athletes: a systematic review and meta-analysis

en.history: Poria cocos (fu ling) is one of the most widely used herbs in Traditional Chinese Medicine, traditionally prescribed to address phlegm and fluid retention, relieve digestive complaints, and calm uneasiness of mind.
en.features: Contains lanostane-type triterpenoids (including pachymic acid) alongside polysaccharides; traditional and modern research has focused on diuretic, renoprotective, and mild sedative/sleep-supportive properties.
pros: A small clinical study of 21 adults with insomnia (mean age 55) given 800 mg of Poria cocos extract nightly found a statistically significant increase in total sleep time, assessed with sleep questionnaires and polysomnography, though the study lacked a placebo control group.

Pharmacological review of Poria cocos triterpenoids describes consistent diuretic and renoprotective activity in laboratory and animal research, aligning with its long traditional use for fluid retention and edema.
cons: The main human sleep study was small, short-term (about two weeks), and not placebo-controlled, so its findings need confirmation in larger controlled trials before being considered strong evidence.

Most of the mechanistic and efficacy evidence for its digestive and fluid-balance effects comes from laboratory and animal studies rather than rigorous human trials, consistent with its classification as traditional use with only limited clinical support.

As with many concentrated herbal diuretics, it may have an additive effect with prescription diuretics or other medications affecting fluid balance -- worth discussing with a healthcare provider if taking such medications, even though no formal contraindication has been established in the current dataset.

en.history: Bupleurum chinense (chai hu) is a foundational herb in Traditional Chinese Medicine, most often used within multi-herb formulas (such as Xiao Chai Hu Tang and Saikokeishikankyoto) for patterns associated with stress, irritability, and "liver qi stagnation."
en.features: Contains saikosaponins, triterpenoid glycosides studied for anti-inflammatory and mood-related activity in animal models; most rigorous human evidence involves multi-herb bupleurum-containing formulas rather than bupleurum in isolation.
pros: A randomized, observer-blinded, controlled trial of the bupleurum-containing kampo formula saikokeishikankyoto in 43 survivors of the 2011 Great East Japan earthquake and tsunami found significantly greater improvement in stress-symptom scores (IES-R) in the treatment group compared with control.

Animal studies of Bupleurum-derived saikosaponins report antidepressant- and anxiolytic-like effects, including changes in hippocampal synaptic signaling in chronic-stress models, though this mechanistic work has not yet been confirmed in dedicated human trials of the isolated herb.
cons: Bupleurum has real, documented case reports of liver toxicity, particularly with prolonged use, high doses, or in combination formulas like Xiao Chai Hu Tang -- a large population study of 639,779 hepatitis B patients found a significantly elevated, dose-dependent risk of liver injury with Radix bupleuri-containing products (adjusted odds ratio 1.90, rising further with cumulative dose). This is a genuine safety concern, not a theoretical one, and anyone with liver disease should avoid it.

Most positive evidence for mood and stress-related symptoms comes from multi-herb formulas or animal studies rather than trials of bupleurum used alone, so standalone human clinical evidence remains limited.
Because of its documented hepatotoxicity risk, use should be short-term and at traditional doses, with liver function monitoring recommended for anyone using it for an extended period.

references.title: Risk of Liver Injury Associated with Chinese Herbal Products Containing Radix bupleuri in 639,779 Patients with Hepatitis B Virus Infection
